speaker: interviewer
text: Day 5, interview with Harriet Solz in Forest Hills, New York. What sort of work did you do after the war? You said you worked your way up to a good position.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, like I said, I went to high school at night. And I had a family. And once the kids got bigger-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] first of all, I was-- I always liked nice things. This is [LAUGHS] my weakness. And we work very hard, so I used--I learned how to become a dress maker. I used to do work at home, do alterations. But once the kids got bigger, I went back for a refresher course in bookkeeping. And I applied for a job. I never finished the course, I got the job. I was hired as an assistant bookkeeper. And I was there eight months. After that, I start wandering, going to better positions, till I worked myself up. I worked very hard. And that was in one company. The people were lazy, they pushed the work on me. But I didn't care, because I was learning all the time. And I worked myself to a position where I was a credit manager. For this, you really need a college degree. I did that without college. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I had beautiful positions. And many times I sat with the people from the banks at the meetings. And I say to myself, look who is sitting here. And those people treated me such respect. If they would know how little education I had, and my background and everything. And I am sitting with all those people from the biggest colleges, with degrees. And I am-- I feel on their level. So really gave me a good feeling.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when the movie came out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sick last year. So I knew about it. I didn't know too much about it. Because I'll to be honest with you, I, I got into a different company, different friends, different crowd. Most of our survivors were much older than me. And they came with families here. I was 18 years old. I, I became friendly with American people. And I wasn't so much-- and since I wanted to forget. So I wasn't so much in touch, what's going on. But later on, through-- because I belong to my society, which I am a member of, Cracow Friendship Society. And to tell you the truth, I belong there because I know I belong there. But still, I feel like an outsider, looking inside. I don't know why, because I was so young, I never kept up with the people. And when you're 18, somebody's 30, it's a big difference in age, with different interests and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I found out, and I was very anxious to see it. And that was right after operation. I don't know how I made it, but I went to see it. I saw three times, the movie. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, Schindler's book came out 10 years prior to the movie. So my daughter-- I didn't want to read it. So my daughter bought the book and was reading. And she called me up to ask questions. I said, Heidi, I-- here name is Heidi. I said, Heidi, I really don't remember a lot. Let me go read the book. And still, I have a lot of questions, which I remembered certain things differently, but I'm not sure. I was too young to-- I looked at things differently and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the movie, I couldn't wait to see it. But when I came out from the movie, I was really disappointed. So my daughter calls me and she says, Ma, you went through so much? You never told me what you went through. You, you-- when you talk, you make like an adventure movie. So I said, Heidi, the movie didn't show nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is nothing, what they showed in the movie. And then I been thinking about that movie a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came to a conclusion. That's not a Holocaust movie. This is a biography of Schindler. If he would have put in more morbid scenes into the picture, people wouldn't have seen it. Like this, that movie woke up the whole world, which he did a marvelous job. But really, the movie is a biography of Schindler, who saved us our lives. And which he deserves it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything, though, that you wish had been in the movie that wasn't, that would be more reflective of what you went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: People wouldn't go-- come to see it, like these people went to see it. Because it's not a Holocaust movie. And there were plenty movies. And you see, none of the movies made such a success like this movie, really. Opened up the eyes and the world and everything. Like we had a commemoration Sunday for my society. So I said-- I saw so many second-generation and third-generation at the commemoration, which we never had. So I said, this is Spielberg's work. We never had it, so many young people to come to commemoration. And Spielberg did it. And my kids know more [LAUGHS] than-- about me, which they didn't know nothing, very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you want to make this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: My kids wanted me to make it, really. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And maybe-- they should know. They'll have-- I never talk about it. I always pushed it to the side. I didn't dwell on it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And my kids were after me. Because like last year, they had so many interviews on TV. I never went to one. And they were really, why don't I go? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But I wasn't feeling well, either. And it's about time. I think the world should know, especially of a child living through such a war. I think it's very meaningful. And things which everybody takes for granted, it is-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to, to me, they were new things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you feel a little better that you finally talked about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's why when my granddaughters-- my son's both girls, they reach the age of 10, which is a very significant age to me-- they were treated like queens, queens of the day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One will-- one will be 13 in April. And the other one was 10 in September. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I told them, why. [LAUGHS] That's my world fell apart, but yours is going to grow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we took them to a show, bought them a corsage, took them to dinner. And they were so proud, walking around with a corsage. People came up-- it's your birthday today-- and made them feel good. And we are a very close-knit, that family. Thank you for making this record.

speaker: interviewee
text: You are welcome. Will they be able to use it? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a wonderful testimony. It's very important.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, I'll signal you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was lucky to obtain some pictures since I was a baby, prior to the war. Because my father had family in the United States, and he was sending them pictures. So this is a picture taken in 1930 in Cracow, me as a baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's all this, Happy 65th birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this. My son and my daughter-in-law made me a surprise birthday party when I was 65. And these are all friends who signed the picture, who were at the party. This picture was taken few weeks before the war broke out. And I am the one in the middle, between my father that my mother. And on the left side is my sister, my younger sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about this day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- we knew-- my father was going to go to the army. And we wanted to have a family picture. We went for a walk in the park, which was a beautiful weather. And a photographer took the picture of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents names and your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, my sister's name is Hermina. My mother's name, Steffa. And my father's name was Wolf. This picture was taken right after the war, in Germany, in Landsberg, when we-- in 1945, when we came from Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am-- my mother's in the center. My sister is on this side, which is the right side.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's the left side.

speaker: interviewee
text: Left side. And I am on the right side. This picture was taken when I bought the first dress in the United States. And I felt so proud, so all dressed up, and a bag to match with the shoes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a big thing in my life, after wearing all those rags.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it taken, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what year it was taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: So shortly after you arrived.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I would say half a year later. Because it was a warm weekend in January. This is a picture of me and my husband when we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this on your wedding day, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: On our wedding day. I am wearing a big corsage. He was wearing a boutonniere.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elias.

speaker: interviewer
text: Want to say the last name, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Elias Solz, S-O-L-Z. OK, this picture was taken in 1972. This was the first time I went to Israel. And I went on a tour to Yad Vashem. And then we walking through the, the Street of the Righteous, whatever they call it, where gentile people plant their tree.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the guy-- the guides, they had done only that time, '72, maybe four, five trees planted by gentiles. So the guide says, this tree was planted by Oskar Schindler. I didn't know from nothing. I gave out such a scream.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, he saved my life. I can't believe it. And I took the picture near his tree. We have two children, lovely kids. This is our son, with his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's-- what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Billy [LAUGHS] and Susan.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. When it taken? A couple years ago. This is our daughter, Heidi, and her husband-- Heidi Solz and her husband, Roger Kerr. This picture was taken half a year ago. These two girls, Janine on the left, and Simone on the right, are our son's children-- our son and daughter-in-law's children. And Janine is 10 years old here, that was on her birthday. And Simone was graduating public school. This was taken last June. 12, she was 12 years old, Simone. These are my-- our daughter, Heidi Solz and Roger Kerr, our two grandchildren. Johanna is four years old. And Daniel is six years old. This was taken around half a year ago. OK, this book was written by Elinor Brecher. She got in touch with people who were by Schindler. And she took their biography. I was-- she called me a few times for interviews. But I was no condition to participate.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was last year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, last year. So when I, I saw the book, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it gave me some chills. Because the cover on the book is a copy of a Schindler list, the way it looked. And first three names are my mother, my sister, and my--

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Offman, O-F-F-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have no idea. Many of them wouldn't have pictures. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Harriet, who are these people who have suddenly appeared?

speaker: interviewee
text: On my right hand side is my husband, Elias. And in the middle is my son, William.

speaker: interviewer
text: Elias, do you want to say a few words to your wife? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not prepared. [LAUGHTER] It's a pleasure to have such a nice son, and such a good wife, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and happy wife, I think so. And we are a happy family, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what we'll be for longer and longer years.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, you've lived with your wife's story. Are you glad that she shared it today? I am very happy. She were waiting for such a occasion. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] All this changed the attitude to, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the-- to the what are left over from Hitler's annihilation. And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] that's all. And William, what do you have to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. By the way, I've been referred to as Bill all these years. I don't know why my mom became so formal all of a sudden, you know. But ever since she's become a star, you know, things have happened. In any event, you know, I'm certainly-- this is an important opportunity, you know, for me to be here. And I'm very grateful. And I'm really obviously very grateful to my mother, because you know, here, you know, before your cameras, is real living proof of a real tragedy to success kind of story, in terms of what she's already indicated to you. She's had to overcome tremendous, tremendous, unbelievable, inhumane kinds of obstacles, to have even approached, you know, this kind of, you know, position. And you know, certainly, I'll-- she'll always have like my unending, you know, gratitude, as well as, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, as well as, you know, a strong feeling. Because needless to say, if not for her courage and her dedication, in terms of her own survival, I wouldn't be here talking to whoever is watching this film.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you know, her story is a true one. And I think that's something that you know, many of you out there may kind of have forgotten, as you look back in your history books. But it's true. And I can only say it's true, because I've heard it, you know, second hand from my mom, and as you end up hearing. But I think hopefully, you know, there are lots of messages that can be gleaned, you know, from her history, as well as the others that you've seen. And hopefully, that will all be helpful in terms of enabling each of you to grow. And certainly, it's made my life more meaningful. And you know, I have a great amount of respect and pride, and you know, I think it's terrific that she's-- also has, you know, the strength and the wherewithal to put her thoughts and feelings, and share this information, you know, with all of you on tape. Any questions?
